steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make the past tense verb
Changed from 'he read' to 'read!' by adding alif at start and making word shorter - this makes it a command
Changed initial alif to a special alif that helps pronunciation

steps.m: to be elevated or high above

steps.n: The root س-م-و meaning 'high' became the noun 'name' (اِسْم). The alif at the start was dropped here. The و at the end also drops when connecting to other words.
Adding alif at the start is common for Arabic nouns. This makes it the complete word for 'name'.
Added بِ at beginning to mean 'by/in' and changed the ending vowel to kasra because it's being acted upon by بِ. The ٱ is just a different way of writing the connecting alif.

steps.m: to care for, nurture, develop to completion

steps.n: The doubled middle letter (shadda) makes this an intensive form, emphasizing the complete nature of the nurturing
The كَ at the end means 'your', making it 'your Lord' instead of just 'Lord'
This is the basic form of the relative pronoun used to connect parts of a sentence, like 'who' in English
Added ٱلّ at the beginning to make it definite. This word connects 'your Lord' (رَبِّكَ) to the next word 'created' (خَلَقَ), showing that it was your Lord who did the creating.

steps.m: to create or bring into existence from nothing
he created (connected to رَبِّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this happened in the past. This word tells us what your Lord (رَبِّكَ) did - He created.

steps.m: to create, form, or shape something
he created (subject of a sentence about الْإِنسٰنَ - mankind)
steps.n: Added fathas to connect the letters and show this is a complete past-tense verb. This word is telling us what Allah did - He created mankind (الْإِنسٰنَ) which comes right after this word.
The root أنس (being social/familiar) gets extra letters to make إنسان meaning 'human' - like how humans are naturally social creatures
Adds ال to specify we're talking about humans in general. This word is what was created (object) in the phrase 'He created the human'
Adds the َ (fatha) at the end to show this word is the object (thing being acted upon) in the sentence

steps.m: from (shows that عَلَقٍ is the source/origin)
steps.n: This is a basic preposition that shows where something came from or what it's made of. In this ayah, it connects عَلَقٍ (clinging substance) as the source material of creation.

steps.m: from (shows that عَلَقٍ is the source/origin)
steps.n: Added sukun (ْ) on the ن to show where the word stops

steps.m: to cling or stick (especially of blood clots)

steps.n: Changed the verb into a noun for a thing that clings - specifically a clot of blood
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show this is indefinite (like 'a' in English) and is the object of the word مِنْ (from) in the phrase 'created man from a blood clot'
Changed root letters into command form by adding marks - a stop mark (sukun) on first letter and short 'a' sound (fatha) on middle letter
Added alif with wasl at start and sukun at end to complete command form. This is the first word revealed of the Quran, commanding Prophet Muhammad ﷺ to read.
Added fatha on first letter to make it a noun
Added damma at the end to show this is the doer in the sentence
Adds second person masculine singular possession. This word is the subject (doer) in the sentence, showing who is most generous.

steps.m: and your Lord (as subject of الْأَكْرَمُ)
steps.n: Adds conjunction prefix meaning 'and'. This word is the subject of الْأَكْرَمُ (most generous) in this ayah.

steps.m: to be generous, noble, or honorable

steps.n: Added vowels to make it mean 'he was' noble/generous in the past
Changed to the pattern that shows something has the highest degree of a quality

steps.m: the Most Noble/Generous (describing رَبُّكَ - your Lord)
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite. This is describing Allah (your Lord) in the ayah.
This is the basic form of the word that points to someone or something. It's like saying 'who' or 'which' in English.

steps.m: The one who (points to Allah in this ayah, who is teaching)
steps.n: Adding ٱلَّ at the beginning makes it more specific, like saying 'the one who' instead of just 'who'. In this ayah, it's referring to Allah who is teaching.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Adding a shadda (doubling mark) on the ل changes the meaning from 'know' to 'teach'
Original meaning comes from cutting/trimming (like cutting a pen from reed)
Transforms root meaning of 'cutting' into concrete noun for writing instrument (since pens were cut from reed)
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific pen

steps.m: by/with the pen (tool used by عَلَّمَ - taught)
steps.n: Adding بِ shows this pen is the tool/means used for teaching. The kasra at the end shows it follows the preposition بِ. This word connects to عَلَّمَ (taught) by showing how the teaching was done.
Adding vowels to make it a past tense verb meaning 'to know'
Doubling the ل changes it from 'know' to 'teach' - like making someone else know
Root means 'to be social' - changed to a collective noun meaning all of humankind
Changes from general mankind to one specific human
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific (like 'the' in English)
Added a fatha (َ) at the end to show this word receives the action of teaching

This is a relative pronoun meaning 'what'. In this ayah, it's the object of the verb عَلَّمَ (taught), showing what was taught to humans. It connects to لَمْ يَعْلَمْ (did not know) which describes these things that were taught.
This is a negative particle that changes the meaning of verbs that come after it. It makes present tense verbs refer to the past and negates them (like saying 'did not' in English).
Adding مْ at the end makes this specifically refer to past events. In this ayah, it makes يَعْلَمْ (know) into 'did not know'.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing vowels

steps.m: he knows/learns (in the phrase 'what he did not know')
steps.n: Added sukun at the end because this word comes after لَمْ (not) which requires the jussive form
This is the basic form of the word that means to stop or prevent something

steps.m: No indeed! (strongly rejecting what came before)
steps.n: Adding the long alif at the end makes this word stronger and turns it into a particle that strongly rejects or corrects what was said before
This is the basic form of the word that shows something might happen
Adding the shadda changes this from a word about possibility to one that shows certainty. It emphasizes the word ٱلْإِنسٰنَ (mankind/human) that comes after it.
Changes the root into a noun that means the whole human species
Changes from general humankind to mean a single human being
Adds ال to make it specific, like saying 'the human' instead of just 'a human'
The fatha at the end shows this word is being acted upon by something in the sentence
Adding fatha vowels between root letters to form past tense pattern فَعَلَ
Adding يَ prefix creates present tense. Second root letter gets sukun
Adding لَ at beginning adds emphasis, showing this is definitely true
Adding alif maksura (ىٰ) at the end is because this word ends with a weak letter ي

steps.m: that (connects to رَّءَاهُ ٱسْتَغْنَىٰٓ - he saw himself self-sufficient)
steps.n: This is a connecting word that introduces what comes next. Here it connects to the fact that someone saw themselves as self-sufficient.

steps.m: that (connects to رَّءَاهُ ٱسْتَغْنَىٰٓ - he saw himself self-sufficient)
steps.n: Added a fatha mark (َ) above the أ to show how to pronounce it correctly as 'anna'

steps.m: to see with eyes or understand with heart

steps.n: Added fathas to show past tense. The final ي changes to ى because it's at the end of a past tense verb.

steps.m: he saw him (object is the person who thinks they don't need Allah)
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who is being seen. The middle letter changes from أ to ء with ا after it when adding endings.

steps.m: he saw him (connected to أَن before it)
steps.n: Added shadda because this word comes after أَن. The shadda shows strong connection between the words.
Adding vowels to show this is a past tense verb describing a state

steps.m: he considered himself free from need
steps.n: Added است at beginning to show someone considering themselves to have this quality

steps.m: he considered himself free from need
steps.n: Added alif maksura to show proper spelling of weak letter ending

steps.m: he considered himself free from need (because رَّءَاهُ - he saw himself)
steps.n: Added elongation mark for pronunciation. This word explains why he acted this way - because he saw himself (رَّءَاهُ).
This is a special word that doesn't come from a root. It's used at the start of sentences to make what follows stronger or more certain.
Added the vowel marks and shadda to show how it's pronounced. This word emphasizes that the return is definitely to your Lord.
This is a basic preposition that shows direction. It doesn't come from a root - this is its simplest form.
Added the little alif (ىٰ) at the end. This is just a writing style and doesn't change the meaning. In this ayah, it connects to رَبِّكَ (your Lord) to show movement towards Him.
Connected the root letters and added shadda to show complete and perfect nurturing
Added kasra at the end to show this word is owned by or connected to something
Added كَ to show this belongs to you (masculine singular). This word follows إِلَىٰ (to) in the ayah.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Changed vowels and added ى to create a noun meaning the place or concept of return
Added ال to make the word definite, meaning 'the specific return' rather than just any return

steps.m: to see or consider something mentally or physically

steps.n: Changed from basic root 'to see' into past tense 'he saw' by adding vowels and writing with ى at the end
Added تَ at the end to change from 'he' to 'you', and brought back the ي with sukun because it's no longer at the end

steps.m: have you seen/considered? (asking about ٱلَّذِى يَنْهَىٰ - the one who forbids)
steps.n: Added أَ at beginning to make it a question, and changed the middle letter to hamza (ء) for proper pronunciation. This is a rhetorical question asking about someone mentioned later in the ayah.
This is the basic form of the word that means 'who' or 'which', used to connect parts of a sentence
Added ال at the beginning to make it specific. This word points to the person who is doing the forbidding (يَنْهَىٰ) in this ayah
Changed spelling to Quranic style: hamza wasl (ٱ), shadda on ل, and ى instead of ي at the end. These are just spelling changes that don't affect the meaning
Added vowel marks to connect the letters and show this happened in the past
Added يَ at the beginning to show this is happening now, and changed the middle vowel to sukun
Added the small alif (ٰ) above the ى to show proper pronunciation
Connected the root letters with vowel marks to form a noun meaning someone who serves/worships
Added ًا at the end to show this is talking about any servant (not a specific one) who is doing the action of praying

This is a basic time word that shows when something happens. It's like saying 'at the time' in English. In this ayah, it connects the time of prayer (صَلَّىٰٓ) with the servant (عَبْدًا) being mentioned.
Adding ا at the end makes it mean 'whenever' instead of just 'when'. It shows this happens repeatedly - every time the servant prays.
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
The doubled ل shows that the prayer was done with extra effort or intensity. The ى at the end is because the root ends in و
The small mark above the ى shows it's pronounced like a short 'aa' sound at the end
The doubled letter shows the prayer was done with extra effort or intensity

steps.m: to see or consider something carefully

steps.n: The root letters got vowels (َ) and the last letter changed to ى to make 'he saw' in the past tense
Added ت at the end to change from 'he' to 'you', and brought back the ي with a sukun (ْ) mark
Added أَ at the beginning to turn the statement into a question, like adding 'have' and '?' in English
This is a conditional particle that introduces a condition. In this ayah, it connects with كَانَ (he was) to form 'if he was'
Added short 'a' sounds (fatha) to each letter to make it mean 'he was' in the past
The middle letter و changes to ا because it's a weak letter between two fathas
This is a preposition that shows something is on top of or upon something else. Here it connects with ٱلْهُدَىٰٓ (guidance) to show someone is upon guidance.
Added damma (ُ) on first letter and fatha (َ) on second letter to make it a noun meaning 'guidance'. The ي changes to ى at the end because it's a special ending for abstract concepts.
Added ٱلْ (al) at the beginning to make it specific. The small alif (ٰ) and mark (ٓ) at the end are pronunciation helpers.
The ال was already there in both words - no real change in meaning. The only difference is in the writing style (hamza and alif symbols)

steps.m: or (connecting two choices in أَمَرَ - he commanded)
steps.n: This is a basic connecting word that gives a choice between two options. In this ayah, it connects two possible things that were commanded.
Basic past tense form of the verb
Added fathas to create past tense verb. This word is followed by بِٱلتَّقْوَىٰٓ (with righteousness) showing what was commanded.
The root letters form the base meaning of protection
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a basic past tense verb
Changed from verb 'guard' to noun meaning 'protecting oneself from sin'
Added ال to make it specific and doubled the ت because it comes after ال
Added بِ at the beginning to show how something is done, and added the long alif (ٰٓ) at the end for emphasis
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to make a past tense verb
Added تَ at the end to change 'he' to 'you'
Added أَ at the beginning to make it a question
This is a conditional particle that introduces the first part of an if-then statement. Here it connects to the word كَذَّبَ (he denied/rejected) to mean 'if he denied/rejected'
The kasra (إِ) under the hamza shows this is the conditional 'if', not the emphatic 'indeed' which would have a fatha (إَ)
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to create the basic past tense verb
Adding shadda (doubling mark) on the middle letter makes the meaning more intense. This word is what was seen/observed in the phrase 'Have you seen the one who...'

steps.m: to be close to or follow

steps.n: Connected the root letters with basic past tense vowels
The word was made stronger by doubling the middle letter (adding shadda), changing the meaning from 'being close' to 'turning away strongly'
Added تَ at beginning to show doing action to oneself
Added و at beginning to connect to previous word
Added alif maksura (ىٰ) with small alif above it for pronunciation
Added أَ at the beginning to turn the statement into a question
Added مْ at the end which adds negation. Together with أَ at the beginning, this creates a question that expects 'yes' as an answer
This is the basic concept of knowing or having knowledge
Added vowel marks (fat-ha) to make it 'he knew' in past tense

steps.m: he knows (subject of يَرَىٰ - he sees)
steps.n: Added يَ at start and changed vowels to show present tense. This word is connected to يَرَىٰ (he sees) in the ayah.
This is the base emphatic particle that shows certainty about what follows
Adding بِ at the beginning changes 'that' to 'because' and connects it to the verb يَعْلَم (he knows) before it in the ayah
Added ال and merged letters to create the unique name for The One True God. The hamza is dropped in this special word.

steps.m: Allah (as the object of يَعْلَم - knows)
steps.n: Allah gets a line over ل and a mark at the end to show something is being known about Allah
Starting with the root that means 'to see'
Connected the root letters with fathas to make the past tense verb
Added يَ at the beginning and changed the vowels to show it's happening now (present tense) instead of already happened (past tense)
The word changes to its normal spelling because of the weak letter rules - this is how we usually write 'he sees'
Added the small alif (ٰ) to show this is a long aa sound
This is the basic form of the word that means to stop or prevent something

steps.m: No indeed! (strongly rejecting what comes after in لَئِن لَّمْ يَنتَهِ)
steps.n: Adding ا at the end makes this word an emphatic particle that strongly rejects or denies what follows. Here it's rejecting the idea that the person won't stop their bad behavior mentioned in the next part of the verse.
Added fatha to show this is the emphasis marker لَ
Added إِنْ which means 'if' to create a conditional statement

steps.m: if indeed (introducing a strong conditional statement)
steps.n: The إِ changes to ئِ when it comes after لَ. This is just a spelling change and doesn't affect the meaning.
Basic negative particle that changes present tense verbs to past negative meaning
Added shadda for emphasis since this comes after لَئِن (indeed if) in the ayah
Added vowel marks (fatha) between root letters and changed the ending to make 'he stopped'
Added اِنْتَ at the beginning to show doing the action to yourself (like stopping yourself)
Added يَ at beginning to show present tense and changed ending vowel to kasra

steps.m: he stops himself (in a conditional phrase)
steps.n: Final ي is dropped because this is in a conditional phrase after لَمْ
This root carries the basic meaning of forcefully grabbing or striking something
Connected the root letters with fatha vowels to make a past tense verb
Added ن at beginning to show 'we' are doing the action, changed vowels for present tense
Added لَ at beginning to show emphasis and future tense

steps.m: surely we two will grab forcefully
steps.n: Added ا at the end to show two people doing the action

steps.m: to grasp or take hold of

steps.n: Changes from basic verb 'to grasp' into 'one who grasps' by following the faa3il pattern

steps.m: forelock (the hair at front of head)
steps.n: The ة makes this refer to a specific body part - the tuft of hair at the front of the head
Adding ال makes it refer to a specific forelock. The ن gets a shadda when ال is added.
The بِ shows this forelock is being grabbed or seized
The ِ at the end shows this word follows the word 'by'

steps.m: to extend forward, protrude (especially about the front of something)

steps.n: Changed the root into a noun by adding ا after ن and ِ after ص to describe something that sticks out or extends forward

steps.m: forelock (the front part of hair that extends forward)
steps.n: Adding ة makes this refer to a specific thing that extends forward - in this case, the forelock (front part of hair)

steps.m: a forelock (which is lying and sinful)
steps.n: The ٍ shows this is being described by the words that follow it (كٰذِبَةٍ 'lying' and خَاطِئَةٍ 'sinful')
Added alif after first letter to make it mean 'someone who lies' (like turning 'lie' into 'liar')
Added ة to make it refer to a female liar instead of a male liar
Added tanwin kasra at the end to show it's describing the forelock (نَاصِيَةٍ) mentioned before it
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Changes from verb (to sin) to noun (sinner) by adding alif after first letter
Added the feminine marker to describe a feminine noun
Added the genitive case ending because it's describing نَاصِيَةٍ (forelock) which is in the genitive case

steps.m: to call, invite, or summon someone

steps.n: Root letters د-ع-و got vowels (fatha) to make past tense 'he'. The و at the end turned into ا, which is normal for this type of word.
Changes from past to present tense by adding يَ at start and changing the ending to و
Added لِ at start to mean 'let him'. The و at the end drops because of the command form.
Added فَ at start to mean 'so/then', and changed لِ to لْ
Added vowels and connected letters to make a noun meaning 'gathering place' or 'assembly'
Added possessive pronoun 'his' and fatha before it to show this is the object being called. This word is what is being called in the phrase 'let him call his gathering place'.

steps.m: to call out to someone or something

steps.n: Changed from root letters to past tense verb by adding vowels (he called)
Added نَ at start to show 'we' are doing the action. The final vowel changes to و because it's happening now, not in the past.

Added سَ at start to show this will happen in the future. The final و drops because it meets another word. This word connects to الزَّبَانِيَةَ (the guards of Hell) which tells us who is being called.

steps.m: to push or drive away forcefully

steps.n: Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the basic past tense verb

steps.m: one who pushes away very forcefully
steps.n: Doubling the middle letter makes the pushing action stronger and more intense
Added special plural ending for groups of powerful beings

steps.m: the angels who push away forcefully
steps.n: Added ال to make it definite, referring to these specific angels. The ز gets a shadda because it comes after ال

steps.m: the angels who push away forcefully (object of سَنَدْعُ)
steps.n: Added fatha at the end because this word is the object of the verb سَنَدْعُ (We will call)
This is the basic form of the word that means 'no'
Adding shadda makes the word stronger

steps.m: No indeed! (used for strong denial or rejection)
steps.n: Adding alif at the end makes it a complete particle that strongly rejects what came before. In this ayah, it's rejecting the previous statements and introducing the command that follows (don't obey him).
This is the basic negative word that means 'no' or 'not'
Adding the fatha mark shows this is being used as a command to stop something. In this ayah, it's telling someone not to obey (تُطِعْهُ).
The root letters show the basic concept of willing obedience
Connected the root letters with vowels to make the past tense verb
Changed from past tense (he obeyed) to present tense (obeying) by changing middle vowel from aa to ii
Added تُ at the beginning to show 'you' are doing the action
Changed from normal present tense 'you obey' to a negative command 'do not obey' by adding sukun at the end

steps.m: you obey him (لَا negates this command: 'do not obey him')
steps.n: Added هُ at the end to show who is being obeyed. This is negated by لَا in the verse.

steps.m: to prostrate, to bow down in worship
steps.n: The root letters show the basic action of prostrating
Connected the root letters with fatha marks to form the past tense verb
Changed from 'he prostrated' to a command meaning 'prostrate!' by adding alif at start and changing the vowels
Added و at beginning to connect this command with what comes before it
Added vowels to connect the letters together
Added اِ at start and ت after first letter to show doing the action actively and with more effort
Added ْ at end to complete the command form
Added وَ (and) at the beginning to connect with previous command
